content.text: Claude in Chrome

is a browser extension that connects your Chrome session to Cowork goals. When the extension is active and a goal references web browsing, the engine directs Chrome to navigate to URLs, read the page content, and optionally follow links — all within your existing browser session, using whatever credentials and cookies you already have.

This makes the Chrome connector qualitatively different from the Drive, Slack, and Gmail connectors. Those connectors authenticate via OAuth and access discrete APIs. Chrome accesses the web as you — logged in, session-aware, able to reach any page your browser can reach, including internal tools that have no API.

The extension is a required component — goals that browse the web only work when the extension is installed and the Cowork desktop or web app is open in the same Chrome profile. Without it, the engine cannot read page content.
How the Chrome Connector Works

When a goal needs to read a web page, the engine sends a directive to the Chrome extension running in your browser. The extension navigates to the specified URL, extracts the page's readable text and structure (not the raw HTML), and streams the content back to the engine. The engine then continues its reasoning with that content in context.
Step-by-step data flow
Goal instruction references a URL or asks to search the web for a topic
Cowork engine sends a navigation directive to the Chrome extension
Extension loads the URL in a background tab (or the active tab if the goal specifies)
Extension waits for the page to fully render (including JavaScript-rendered content)
Extension extracts the page text, headings, links, and visible structured data
Extracted content is sent back to the engine for reasoning
Engine can direct the extension to follow a specific link or load another URL, continuing the chain
Session and authentication

The extension runs in your Chrome profile. It inherits your login sessions, saved credentials, and cookies — exactly as if you had opened the page yourself. This means a goal can read your company's internal Confluence wiki, your SaaS dashboard at a tool with no API, or a site behind SSO — as long as you are already logged in when the goal runs.
No credentials are shared with Cowork — the engine never sees your passwords or session tokens
The extension reads what the rendered page shows; it does not have deeper access than a logged-in user would
If a page requires 2FA or a CAPTCHA, the goal will pause and you will be prompted to complete it manually
Incognito tabs are out of scope unless you have explicitly enabled the extension in incognito mode
What the Chrome Connector Can Read

The extension extracts the visible, rendered content of a page — not the raw HTML source. This distinction matters: pages built with heavy JavaScript that load content dynamically are readable because the extension waits for rendering to complete before extracting text.
Readable content types
Content type
Accessible?
Notes
Static text and headings
Yes
All rendered paragraph and heading text
JavaScript-rendered content
Yes
Extension waits for DOM to settle before extracting
Tables with visible data
Yes
Row and column structure is preserved
Page links and href targets
Yes
Used for follow-link directives
Images
No
Alt text is read; image pixels are not
PDF files opened in browser
Partial
Text layer extracted if browser PDF viewer renders it
Video and audio content
No
Media files are not transcribed
Content behind a modal or login gate
Only if logged in
Extension uses your session; gated content requires auth
Infinite scroll / lazy-loaded content
Partial
Extraction happens at current scroll position; goal must direct scrolling explicitly
What the extension does not do
It does not execute arbitrary JavaScript in page context — it reads the rendered DOM, not the JS runtime
It does not capture screenshots or visual representations of the page
It does not interact with browser extensions other than itself
It does not access browser history, bookmarks, or downloads outside a goal-directed navigation
Writing Goals That Use Chrome

Goals reference the Chrome connector implicitly — you describe what web content you need, and the engine decides when to invoke the extension. You do not need to include a connector name in the goal text; mentioning a URL or a web search task is sufficient.
Example: web research brief

Task goal:
Research the current state of model context windows across the five largest
LLM providers. For each provider, find:
  - Their flagship model name
  - Stated maximum context window (in tokens)
  - Any published retrieval-augmented search capability

Use their official documentation or announcement pages — not third-party
summaries. List each source URL.

Write the findings to a new Google Doc called "Context Window Comparison — [date]"
and share it with research-team@company.com.

The engine will search the web, load each provider's documentation site, extract the relevant data, and synthesize the brief. The Drive connector handles the output. Both connectors work in sequence in one goal.
Example: monitor a competitor pricing page

Task goal (scheduled, runs every Monday at 09:00):
Load the pricing page at https://competitor.example.com/pricing.
Extract the plan names, monthly prices, and included feature list for each tier.
Compare this to the baseline stored in "competitor-pricing-baseline.json" in
the shared project folder on Drive.

If any price or feature has changed:
  - Update the baseline file with today's date and new values
  - Post a summary of changes to #competitive-intel in Slack

If nothing has changed, do nothing.
This pattern uses Chrome to read a web page, Drive to read and write the baseline file, and Slack to post an alert — three connectors in a single goal triggered on a schedule.
Example: extract structured data from a web list

Task goal:
Navigate to https://internal-tools.company.com/open-requests and read the
list of open support requests.
For each request, extract: ID, title, submitted date, and assigned team.
Write the results to the "Open Requests" sheet in Google Drive, appending
new rows below the existing data.
Stop after 5 pages of results or if the list is shorter than 5 pages.

Because the internal tool is accessible via the user's existing login session, no additional authentication configuration is needed. The goal works as long as the user is logged in when the scheduled task runs.
Directing the Extension: URLs and Searches

Goals can direct the Chrome connector in two ways: specifying a URL directly, or asking the engine to search for a topic. In the search case, the engine formulates a search query, loads the results page, and selects links to follow based on relevance.
Direct URL navigation
Include the full URL in the goal text: the engine treats it as the primary source
For pagination, describe the pattern: "navigate to page 2, page 3" or "follow the Next button until no more results"
For site-scoped research: "search within docs.example.com for topic X" — the engine uses a site: search operator
For link-following depth: "follow up to 3 links from the landing page" — the engine stops at that depth
Web search navigation
Describe what you need and the engine formulates search queries automatically
Specify a source quality preference: "use official documentation", "peer-reviewed sources only", "published after 2024"
Specify a maximum number of sources: "read the top 5 results" — prevents unbounded browsing
The engine cites each source URL it used in its output, so you can verify the data origin
Browsing depth and cost

Each page load is a round-trip between the engine and the extension. Deep link-following (5+ hops) or wide searches (10+ sources) take proportionally more time. For time-sensitive goals, limit browsing depth explicitly in the goal text.

A good rule: for research that needs authoritative data, specify the exact source URLs if you know them. Reserve open web search for exploratory tasks where the goal is to discover what sources exist.
Security and Privacy Model

The Chrome connector is the most sensitive connector in Cowork because it can access any page your browser can reach. Understanding its security model is important before using it for tasks that touch sensitive data.
What Cowork sees and does not see
Data type
Does Cowork see it?
Notes
Page text and headings
Yes
Sent to the engine for reasoning
Your login session cookies
No
Never sent; extension acts on your behalf
Your passwords or autofill data
No
Browser autofill is inaccessible to extensions
Browsing history outside a goal
No
Extension only tracks navigation directed by a goal
Content on pages not visited by a goal
No
Extension is passive unless a goal directs it
Extracted page content
Yes
Included in goal context; subject to Cowork privacy policy
Limiting Chrome scope in a goal
You can scope the Chrome connector tightly in a goal by naming specific URLs or domains rather than allowing open web search. This reduces the chance that the engine reads unintended pages.

Restrict browsing scope in a goal:
Only read pages within docs.mycompany.com and status.mycompany.com.
Do not follow links outside these domains.
Domain restrictions are enforced by the engine's goal reasoning, not by the extension itself
For goals handling confidential data, test with a scoped goal first before widening to open web search
Cowork workspace admins can restrict which connectors are available to team members
Installing and Enabling the Extension
The Chrome extension is installed separately from the Cowork desktop app. It is available in the Chrome Web Store under "Claude in Chrome" and requires no additional configuration after installation.
Install the extension from the Chrome Web Store
Click the extension icon and sign in with the same Anthropic account used for Cowork
The extension status indicator turns green — it is now active
Open the Cowork desktop or web app in the same Chrome profile
Run a goal that references a URL — the extension handles the rest automatically
Requirements and limitations
Chrome version 110 or later — Chromium-based browsers (Edge, Brave) are not officially supported
The Cowork app and Chrome must be open simultaneously for the connector to work
The extension is bound to one Chrome profile — if you use multiple profiles, install and sign in on each separately
Mobile Chrome is not supported — extension support requires the desktop browser
If the extension is disabled or the browser is closed, goals that need to browse will pause and wait
Common Chrome Connector Patterns
Pattern
Goal description
Other connectors
Typical duration
Web research brief
Search web for topic, read N sources, synthesise
Drive (output)
5–15 min
Competitor monitoring
Load pricing/features page, compare to baseline, alert if changed
Drive (baseline), Slack (alert)
2–5 min
Data extraction
Navigate paginated list, extract structured fields, write to sheet
Drive (output)
5–20 min
Internal tool readout
Load internal dashboard, extract current KPIs, post digest
Slack or Drive
2–5 min
Documentation search
Search docs.example.com for a feature, return relevant sections
None (inline output)
1–3 min
Link audit
Load a web page, extract all links, check each for status code
Drive (report)
5–10 min
Chrome vs API Connectors: When to Use Each

App connectors (Drive, Slack, Gmail) connect to a service via its official API. The Chrome connector reads web pages through the browser. These two approaches suit different situations — choosing the right one for a task leads to faster, more reliable goals.
Dimension
API connector (Drive/Slack/Gmail)
Chrome connector
Authentication
OAuth — authorised once in Cowork settings
Your existing browser session
Data freshness
API response at time of call
Rendered page at time of navigation
Reliability
High — structured API response
Medium — depends on page structure changes
Scope
Exactly what the API exposes
Any page your browser can reach
Speed
Fast (direct API call, no rendering)
Slower (browser load + render + extract)
Best for
Structured data in supported apps
Web pages, internal tools, sites without an API
Rate limits
API-level quotas per connector
Depends on target site; no Cowork-imposed limit

Use an API connector when the target service has one and exposes the data you need — it will always be faster and more reliable than reading the web page. Use Chrome for everything else: competitor pages, internal tools, documentation sites, or any service without a Cowork connector.
Troubleshooting the Chrome Connector

Most Chrome connector failures fall into one of four categories: the extension is not running, the page did not render in time, the user is not logged in to the target site, or the page structure has changed and the extraction produced empty content.
Extension not detected
Check that the extension appears in Chrome's toolbar and shows a green status dot
Ensure you are signed in to the extension with the same account used for Cowork
If the Cowork desktop app was opened before the extension was installed, restart the app
The extension must be active in the same Chrome profile that is running the Cowork session
Empty or partial page content
Some pages require scrolling to load all content — add explicit scroll instructions to the goal
Pages with heavy lazy-loading may need a wait instruction: "wait 3 seconds after load before reading"
If a page is behind a gate (login, paywall, or CAPTCHA), the extension will return partial content
Single-page apps (React, Vue) occasionally render after a delay — if content is missing, retry the goal
Goal pauses awaiting manual action

If a page requires 2FA, a CAPTCHA, or an explicit login step after a session expiry, the goal pauses and displays a prompt in the Cowork interface. Complete the browser action, then resume the goal. The engine retries the page read once you signal it to continue.
Page structure changed between goal runs

For goals that run on a schedule and rely on specific page structure (extracting a table row, reading a specific element), a site redesign can break the extraction. If a scheduled goal starts returning empty or garbled data, re-run it manually and inspect what the extension now sees. Update the goal's extraction instructions to match the new layout.
Advanced: Chrome and Scheduled Goals

The Chrome connector works fully in scheduled goals. The extension must be active in an open browser window at the scheduled run time — if Chrome is closed, the goal will queue and wait until the extension reconnects, then run.
Designing robust scheduled browsing goals

Robust scheduled Chrome goal example:
Load https://status.service.example.com.
Extract the current operational status of each component listed on the page.
If the page fails to load or returns an error, write a placeholder entry
to the status log in Drive noting the failure — do not skip the run silently.

If any component is listed as "Degraded" or "Outage":
  Post an alert to #ops-alerts in Slack with the component name and status text.
Otherwise, write the status snapshot to the "status-log" Sheet without posting.
Always include a fallback instruction for page load failures — scheduled goals should never produce silent gaps in logs
Avoid goals that require user interaction (clicking buttons, filling forms) in scheduled context — those steps need a live user present
For pages that require login, ensure the Chrome session does not expire between scheduled runs; use a session-keeping browser setting if necessary
Test scheduled Chrome goals manually first by running the goal at-will before committing it to a recurring schedule
Goal example: weekly link-rot audit

Task goal (scheduled, runs every Sunday at 08:00):
Load the "External Resources" Google Sheet and read all URLs in column B.
For each URL, navigate to the page in Chrome and check whether the page
loads successfully (HTTP 200) or returns an error (4xx, 5xx, or unreachable).
Write the results back to column C ("Status") and column D ("Checked Date").
If more than 5 URLs are broken, post a summary to #web-team in Slack.
This audit goal uses Chrome to test each URL and Drive to read the input list and write results — a complete maintenance workflow that runs without manual intervention.
Summary

The Chrome connector gives Cowork goals live access to the web through your existing browser session. Unlike API-based connectors, it reads any page your browser can reach — public sites, authenticated tools, internal dashboards — without credential sharing.
Requires the Claude in Chrome extension installed and signed in to your Cowork account
Reads rendered page content including JavaScript-loaded elements; does not access images, video, or audio
Inherits your browser login sessions — no additional authentication for pages you are already logged in to
Goals reference web content by URL or by describing a search topic; the engine formulates queries and follows links
Pairs naturally with Drive (research output) and Slack (monitoring alerts) for end-to-end web-to-action workflows
Scope browsing explicitly in goal text to keep sensitive data tasks under tight control
